coredata.dc:title: A traceable nanoplatform for enhanced chemo-photodynamic therapy by reducing oxygen consumption
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Hanchun
Zhang, Suge
Guo, Xiaofang
Li, Ying
Ren, Jinjin
Zhou, Huijuan
Du, Bin
Zhou, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Tumor hypoxia impedes the efficiencies of oxygen-dependent photodynamic therapy (PDT) and chemotherapy. Herein, we design a traceable nanoplatform (DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs) by reducing oxygen (O2) consumption to overcome the hypoxia-caused cancer therapy. Carbon dots (CDs) are used not only as a PDT agent but also applied for in vivo traceable imaging. Metformin (Met), a potent antihyperglycemic agent, to improve tumor oxygenation and enhance the efficiencies of hypoxia-caused cancer therapy. In the hypoxic tumor microenvironment, Met was released more rapidly than DOX, which is advantageous for improving hypoxic cancer to exert a better therapeutic efficiency. Ex vivo immunofluorescence staining revealed that the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles greatly reduce O2 consumption in tumor site. Followed by in vivo synergistic treatment achieved considerably enhanced cancer therapeutic efficiency. This system holds great clinical promise as a traceable imaging approach to guide the improvement of PDT and chemotherapy efficiencies through utilizing a simple, safe method improved hypoxic tumor microenvironment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor hypoxia
Metformin
Carbon dots
Traceable
Enhanced chemo-photodynamic therapy

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Theranostics, Tumor Hypoxia, PDT, Metformin, Carbon Dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of hypoxia in MCF-7/ADR cells
Synthesis and characterizations of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles
In vitro DOX and Met release from the nanoparticles
Cellular uptake and evaluation of hypoxia
DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs alleviate tumor hypoxia in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A scheme showing the preparation of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles and their synergistic effects against breast cancer.

Characterization of nanoparticles. The TEM image of (A) CDs, (C) HA-CDs and (E) DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs; DLS analysis results of (B) CDs, (D) HA-CDs and (F) DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs; (G) UV–vis absorption spectra. (a) CDs; (b) HA-CDs; (c) BSA-HA-CDs; (d) DOX/Met/ BSA-HA-CDs; (H) FTIR spectrum of (a) HA; (b) BSA; (c) CDs; (d) HA-CDs; (e) BSA-HA-CDs;

(A) ROS production of CDs using ROS marker 1,3­diphenylisobenzofuran under irradiation at 1 W/cm2 using 532 nm laser for 10 min. (B) ROS production of CDs, BSA-HA-CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs using ROS marker diphenylisobenzofuran under irradiation at 1 W/cm2 using 532 nm laser for 10 min. (C) Detection of intracellular ROS by DCFH-DA staining in MCF-7 cells incubated with BSA-HA-CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs at different times under 532 nm laser irradiation at 1 W/cm2.
In vitro viability assay toward MCF-7 (A, 48 h) and MCF-7/ADR (B, 48 h) under hypoxia. (n = 6, ** denotes P < 0.01 significant difference).

Fluorescence microscopic images of (A) MCF-7 cells treated with DOX, and DOX/Met/BSA-HA with a DOX concentration of 5 μg/mL at 37 °C for 4 h, and (B) MCF-7/ADR cells treated with DOX, and DOX/Met/BSA-HA with a DOX concentration of 5 μg/mL at 37 °C for 12 h. Red and blue colors represent the fluorescence of DOX and nucleus stained by DAPI, respectively. (C) Western blot analysis of HIF-1α and P-gp expression in MCF-7/ADR cell lines. (D) Semi-quantitative analysis of percentage in (C).

(A) In vivo red emissive fluorescence images of the CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs at different times; (B) The fluorescence images of the isolated organs after 4 h injection; (C) The CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs in various tissues were calculated as the average fluorescent intensity.

Effect of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles on tumor HIF-1α: Representative immunofluorescence images of tumors treated with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles or saline on day 2. (B) Semi-quantification of HIF-1α and blood vessel densities from the images shown in (A).

In vivo therapy. (A) Changes of tumor growth and (B) body weight of mice after various treatments; Data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 6, V0: initial tumor volume) (C) H&E stained histology staining of major organ slices (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumor slices harvested from the mice with different treatments. (D) Representative photographs of tumors after the treatments. (n = 6, ** denotes P < 0.01 significant difference).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.
This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81503038).

head.ce:title: A traceable nanoplatform for enhanced chemo-photodynamic therapy by reducing oxygen consumption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of New Drug Research and Safety Evaluation, Henan Province, Zhengzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of New Drug Research and Safety Evaluation

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, Collaborative Innovation Center of New Drug Research and Safety Evaluation, Henan Province, Zhengzhou, Key Laboratory of Targeting Therapy and Diagnosis for Critical Diseases, Henan Province, Zhengzhou.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, Collaborative Innovation Center of New Drug Research and Safety Evaluation, Henan Province, Zhengzhou, Key Laboratory of Targeting Therapy and Diagnosis for Critical Diseases

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tumor hypoxia impedes the efficiencies of oxygen-dependent photodynamic therapy (PDT) and chemotherapy. Herein, we design a traceable nanoplatform (DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs) by reducing oxygen (O2) consumption to overcome the hypoxia-caused cancer therapy. Carbon dots (CDs) are used not only as a PDT agent but also applied for in vivo traceable imaging. Metformin (Met), a potent antihyperglycemic agent, to improve tumor oxygenation and enhance the efficiencies of hypoxia-caused cancer therapy. In the hypoxic tumor microenvironment, Met was released more rapidly than DOX, which is advantageous for improving hypoxic cancer to exert a better therapeutic efficiency. Ex vivo immunofluorescence staining revealed that the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles greatly reduce O2 consumption in tumor site. Followed by in vivo synergistic treatment achieved considerably enhanced cancer therapeutic efficiency. This system holds great clinical promise as a traceable imaging approach to guide the improvement of PDT and chemotherapy efficiencies through utilizing a simple, safe method improved hypoxic tumor microenvironment.

In this work, we utilized metformin (Met), a potent antihyperglycemic agent, to improve tumor oxygenation and subsequently enhance the efficiencies of hypoxia-caused photodynamic therapy (PDT) and chemotherapy. Carbon dots (CDs) can be applied as a PDT agent for simultaneous traceable imaging and cancer treatment. In the hypoxic tumor microenvironment, Met was released rapidly than DOX, which is advantageous for improving hypoxic cancer to exert a better therapeutic efficiency. Therefore, the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles hold great clinical promise as a traceable imaging approach to guide the improvement of PDT and chemotherapy efficiencies through utilizing a simple, safe strategy to improve hypoxic tumor microenvironment.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Tumor hypoxia, resulting from an imbalance between the supply and consumption of oxygen (O2), is a characteristic feature of solid tumors. 1,2 Available O2 is consumed by irregular cancer cells proliferation at the tumor periphery, thereby limiting the diffusion of O2 into the deep layers of tumors, causing local hypoxia as well as acidulated environment. 3 It is well documented that the occurrence of hypoxia is associated with most medical therapies, particularly radiotherapy and photodynamic therapy (PDT). 3–5 PDT is a well-known non-invasive approach utilizing photosensitizers, generating cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the presence of O2 to kill tumor cells. 6–8 With the hypoxia in tumor tissue, continuous O2 consumption would severely hamper the efficacy of PDT. 9 Furthermore, studies have demonstrated that hypoxic tumor microenvironment correlates closely with multi-drug resistance, while multi-drug resistance hinders the efficiency of chemotherapy, such as methotrexate and doxorubicin (DOX). 10,11 Cellular response to hypoxia is believed to be hypoxia-inducible factor-1 (HIF-1), which leads to the transcription of multidrug resistance gene 1 (MDR1). HIF-1 is a heterodimer consisting of a HIF-1α and HIF-1β subunit. 12 Recent studies have confirmed a close connection between chemoresistance and HIF-1α expression in multiple malignancies, its expression and transcriptional activity decreasing exponentially with increases in cellular O2 levels. 11,13 Therefore, to elevate the O2 level in hypoxia tumor microenvironments act as a potential therapeutic strategy to overcome hypoxia limitations for effective cancer therapy.

Today, investigators are increasingly concentrated on overcoming tumor hypoxia and improving cancer treatment, including enhanced PDT and chemotherapy. For example, Luo et al used hemoglobin-based oxygen-carrier to deliver more O2 for enhanced PDT. 14 Tang et al utilized oxygen-carrier (perfluorocarbon), which has a high O2 capacity to enrich O2 for boosted photodynamic strategy. 15 Zhang et al used MnO2 modulate hypoxic tumor microenvironment for reversing MDR in resistant tumor. 10 In a word, most of the current researches focus on providing O2 supply to overcome tumor hypoxia. However, it has been reported that the extent of hypoxic regions depends on blood flow rate, blood O2 content and the tumor's O2 consumption rate. Secomb et al presented theoretical simulations O2 transport, suggesting that reducing O2 consumption may be more effective at improving tumor oxygenation than elevating blood flow or increasing O2 supply to reduce tumor hypoxia. 16 Therefore, a safe, simple and effective strategy to achieve improved the O2 supply to the tumor is to decrease cellular O2 consumption. Metformin (Met) (1,1-dimethylbiguanide hydrochloride) is a potent antihyperglycemic agent, which has already been approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). 17 The primary mechanism of Met is considered to be a potent respiration inhibitor by inhibiting the activity of complex I in the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC) and impairs mitochondrial oxidation. 18,19 Recently, researchers found that free Met could efficiently increase in tumor radiotherapy response by inhibiting tumor cells O2 consumption and improving tumor oxygenation. 20 Based on these encouraging previous studies, in order to better improve tumor hypoxia and therapeutic efficiencies, drug delivery systems-mediated PDT and chemotherapy by reducing tumor O2 consumption remain to be required.

In recent years, imaging-guided, visualized cancer therapy has been a rapidly developing field to enhance therapeutic efficiency and reduce side effects and it may become one of the mainstream cancer treatments. 21–23 Carbon dots (CDs), emerging significant nanomaterials, have received considerable concern due to their unique properties, such as high stability, high water dispersibility, excellent biocompatibility and photostability. 24,25 To date, CDs have many potential applications in bio-imaging, 26,27 nano-medicine 28–30 and sensors. 31 Xu et al synthesized fluorescent aspirin-based CDs for cellular imaging, bioimaging and anti-inflammation. 26 However, the photoluminescence emission spectra of the known CDs mainly centered in the blue and green region, which is not effective for biological applications because of their limited penetration depth to biosystems. 32,33 In addition, in most studies, CDs were usually used as fluorescent agents; only a few studies concentrated on their intrinsic theranostic applications. Thus, to develop multi-functional CDs for bio-imaging and therapy is a new trend for their biomedical application. Our research group synthesized the CDs can emit red fluorescence, which can be a powerful diagnostic tool with deep tissue traceable imaging. More significantly, the obtained CDs could generate ROS under laser irradiation, which can be used as a PDT agent for efficient cancer treatment. 34 Compared with the traditional organic PDT agents (porphyrin derivative, Ce6), CDs applied as a PDT agent exhibited good water dispersibility, high photo-stability and excellent biocompatibility. 35,36 Therefore, the produced red fluorescence CDs can be applied as a PDT agent for simultaneous traceable imaging and cancer treatment.

In order to better improve tumor hypoxia, a safe and simple strategy to enhance tumor oxygenation and achieve synergistic therapy on PDT and chemotherapy was proposed. In this study, we integrated PDT and chemotherapy, CDs-based traceable imaging into one system, by reducing O2 consumption for high efficient therapy of hypoxic tumor (Figure 1 ). Firstly, CDs and hyaluronic acid (HA) synthesized HA-CDs conjugates, which were used for target tumor cells surface markers such as cluster determinant 44 (CD44) receptors. 37,38 Then, HA-CDs were linked to the bovine serum albumin (BSA); the albumin is chosen due to its nontoxic, biocompatible, and biodegradable properties, which are favorable for drug delivery. 39 Finally, simultaneously encapsulated drugs of DOX and Met in BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles were utilized for highly efficient therapy and traceable imaging of hypoxic tumor. This system (DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs) has the following characteristics. (1) The dual-drug nanoparticles enabled a temporal release of drugs: Met was released rapidly to reduce the tumor O2 consumption, while DOX was released in a sustained manner, so the dual-drug system is advantageous for improving hypoxic tumor. (2) Higher efficacy of the PDT and lower resistance for anticancer drug successfully facilitate the outcomes of synergistic antitumor. (3) The red fluorescence of CDs can be a traceable tool with deep tissue imaging.
Details of the experiments about preparation and characterization of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs were described in the Supplementary data.

Cellular uptake was observed by fluorescence microscopy (Zeiss LSM 510, Thornwood, NY, USA). Cells were seeded in 6-well culture plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well and allowed to adhere for 24 h. Then cells were exposed to 5 μg/mL equivalent DOX solution and DOX/Met/BSA-HA. For MCF-7 cells (Human breast carcinoma cell lines), after incubation for 4 h, the drug-containing solutions were removed and cells washed with PBS for twice and then fixed with 4% (wt/vol) formaldehyde for 15 min. Afterwards, cell nucleus was dyed with DAPI (10 μg/mL) for another 15 min and the cells were washed with PBS for 3 times. The as-prepared samples were imaged by a fluorescence microscope. For MCF-7/ADR cells (Human anti-doxorubicin breast cancer cell lines), after incubation for 12 h, the cells were imaged using the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of hypoxia in MCF-7/ADR cells
ce:section.ce:para: MCF-7/ADR cells were cultured under hypoxia or normoxia conditions for 24 h for attachment before the assay. Then cells cultured under hypoxia condition were divided into two groups, which were treated with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs or fresh culture medium for another 12 h, respectively. The cells in normoxia condition were treated with fresh culture medium. For western blotting assay, the cells were harvested and lysed with radioimmunoprecipitation (RIPA) buffer. Protein extracts were loaded onto a 10% sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel and electrophoresed. Then samples were transferred to a polyvinylidene difluoride membrane and blocked with 5% skim milk for 1 h at room temperature. Afterwards, the samples were incubated with primary antibodies (HIF-1α, P-gp, and β-actin, 1:1000) overnight at 4 °C, followed by incubation with horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-conjugated anti-rabbit secondary antibody (1:3000) at room temperature for 1.5 h. Finally, labeled bands were detected by chemical luminescence.

Animal experiments: KM (Kunming) female mice (18-20 g) were obtained from Henan Laboratory Animal Center. All animal experiments were carried out under protocols approved by Henan Laboratory Animal Center. The in vivo experiments were conducted on S180 ascitic tumor models, which were generated by subcutaneous injection 1 × 106 ascitic tumor cells in 0.1 mL saline into the right flank of KM female mice. And about a week later, when the tumor volumes reached about 100 mm3, the tumor-bearing mice were subjected to the following experiments.

For in vivo real-time fluorescent imaging and evaluated tumor-targeting ability, the mice tumor-bearing were intravenously (i.v.) injected with CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs (200 μL, 3 mg/kg). After injection using an In Vivo Imaging System (IVIS) (Xenogen, Alameda, CA, USA) to collect the fluorescence signals of CDs, fluorescence image data of tumors were taken at 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, and 24 h, respectively. At 4 h after treatments, the mice were dissected, and their organs including heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor were harvested and ex vivo imaging was performed.

To assess the tumor hypoxia alleviating ability of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs in vivo, the mice bearing S180 tumors were randomly divided into two groups, and i.v. injected with saline or DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs (200 μL, dose of Met = 1.5 mg/mL), respectively. The tumors were dissected at 24 h after injection and cut into 5 μm sections for further staining. For the detection of HIF-1α, the tumor slides were incubated with mouse monoclonal HIF-1α antibody (dilution 1:100) and with 488-conjugated goat anti-mouse secondary antibody. For CD31 staining, rat anti-mouse CD31 antibody (dilution 1:100) and Cy3-conjugated goat anti-rat antibody were used as the primary and secondary antibodies, respectively. Finally, the nucleus was stained with DAPI. The fluorescent signals were analyzed by fluorescence microscopy.

In vivo synergetic therapy experiments were performed by S180 tumor model; the tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into nine groups (6 mice per group): (1) saline, (2) BSA-HA-CDs, (3) Met/BSA-HA-CDs, (4) BSA-HA-CDs + laser, (5) Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser, (6) DOX, (7) DOX/BSA-HA-CDs, (8) DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs and (9) DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser (DOX dose = 5 mg/kg, Met dose = 15 mg/kg). All groups were administrated via the tail vein every other day. The PDT experiments were exposed to the 532 nm laser (1 W·cm−2, laser-treated groups) for 5 min at 4 h after injection. The measurements of mice body weight and tumor sizes were performed every 2 days, and the tumor volume could be calculated as volume = 0.5 × L × W2 (L indicates long diameter; W indicates short diameter). After 14 days of treatment, the mice were sacrificed to collect organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) and tumors, and the tumors of each group were isolated and photographed. Then the collected tissues were soaked in a 10% formalin solution for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and observed by a microscope.

Further details of the experiments about preparation, characterization of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs, ROS detection, kinetics of DOX and Met release and cell cytotoxicity can be found in the Supplementary Materials.

In previous studies, CDs were synthesized by using citric acid as a carbon precursor and formamide was chose as a modifier to provide amino group for CDs. The as-produced CDs were water-dispersible, and the color of its solution was dark brown. The morphology of the CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). From Figure 2 , A, it can be seen that the CDs had a uniform dispersion without any apparent aggregation, and the diameters of particles were approximately 5.5 nm. The result of DLS analysis (Figurre 1, B) also demonstrated that the average size of CDs was about 5.5 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs were shown in Figure 2, G; it can be seen that the absorption peak of CDs was at 350 nm, which does accord with the related reports in the literature, indicating the completion of CDs synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image and DLS analysis of HA-CDs were shown in Figure 2, C and D, and the size of conjugates was about 25 nm. Figure 2, E and F showed the representative TEM image and DLS analysis of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles, and the size of nanoparticles was about 170 nm. Figure 2, G showed the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs, HA-CDs, BSA-HA-CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs. CDs exhibited a strong absorption at 350 nm, and it showed unusually broad UV–vis absorption band in the range of 400-500 nm. While the broad band at nearly 500-600 nm can be seen in the absorption spectrum of HA-CDs, the typical band red shift indicated the conjugation of CDs to HA. For BSA-HA-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum displayed a characteristic absorption peak of BSA at 270 nm, which suggested the successful construct of BSA-HA-CDs. The loading of DOX was confirmed by UV–vis spectroscopy with the maximum absorbance peak of DOX observed at 480 nm. As shown in the FT-IR spectra in Figure 2, H, the spectrum of CDs had the characteristic peaks of -NH2 at 1635 cm−1 (–NH) and another band at 1345 cm−1 (–CN), which indicated the existence of amino groups on the surface of pure CDs. The peaks at 1617 and 3385 cm−1 correspond to the carboxylate group (C=O) and the hydroxyl group (–OH) of pure HA. After conjugation of CDs to HA, the amide bond (I) at 1636 cm−1 and amide bond (II) at 1563 cm−1 of the –CONH group appeared. All the typical peaks confirm the conjugate formation successfully by the amidation process. The FT-IR spectrum of BSA-HA-CDs conjugate was similar with that of BSA, which had the characteristic peaks of amide bonds at 1352, 1545, and 1645 cm−1, and the peak around 1043 cm−1 on the FT-IR spectrum of BSA-HA-CDs conjugates appeared corresponding to the stretching vibration of HA in the conjugates. These results confirmed the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles were successfully synthesized.

As-produced CDs displayed excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission characteristic from 500 to 620 nm. It is notable from Supplementary Figure S1, A and B that the fluorescent spectra of CDs showed that the emission wavelength of the CDs increased with the increasing of excitation wavelength from 500 to 620 nm. When the excitation wavelength increased to 620 nm, the maximum emission wavelength was 640 nm. Moreover, this emission phenomenon was easily observed by the naked eye as well (Figure S1, C), which directly confirms the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence of the CDs. Figure S1, D and E showed the fluorescence emission spectra of BSA-HA-CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs at 600 nm, which indicate the fluorescence properties of CDs still exist in these nanoparticles.

The optical absorbance of DPBF solution containing different concentrations of CDs under 1 W·cm−2 of 532 nm laser irradiation was investigated. According to the decay curves of DPBF absorption (Figure 3 , A), in the presence of different concentrations CDs, the DPBF absorption can be regulated to decreased. On the contrary, the absorption of DPBF did not decrease significantly in control group at different times and no ROS was produced in the process, reflecting that CDs had the characteristic of producing ROS. From Figure 3, B, the degradation rate of DPBF absorbance in the presence of CDs, BSA-HA-CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs was decreasing similarly with prolonged irradiation time, indicating the production of ROS will not be obviously influenced when DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles were formed.

In order to prove the ROS generation, the intracellular ROS levels were observed in MCF-7 cells incubated with BSA-HA-CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs through DCFH­DA as a fluorescent indicator. As shown in Figure 3, C, compared with the control and no laser irradiation groups, BSA-HA-CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs groups under 532 nm laser irradiation for 5 min and 10 min showed obvious ROS generation. It is worth noting that the fluorescence of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs was brighter, suggesting that Met may reduce O2 consumption and promote PDT to increase production of ROS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro DOX and Met release from the nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The drug loading and releasing capabilities of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs were investigated. Met and anticancer DOX were chosen to evaluate the loading and controlled release behaviors; the Met encapsulation and loading efficacy were calculated to be 42.9% and 6.6%, respectively. At the studied condition, DOX encapsulation and loading efficacy were calculated to be 89.1% and 4.57%, respectively.

Figure S2, A showed the accumulative release curves of DOX from the nanoparticles. In neutral buffer solution (pH 7.4), a small amount of DOX (17.97%) was released from DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs with 24 h. At pH 5.0, the DOX showed a faster release rate, and the released total amount of DOX reached up to 55.16% at the first 24 h. It has been reported that the electrostatic interaction between DOX and BSA at acidic pH could facilitate the release of DOX. 40 Therefore, the anticancer drug DOX can release quickly in tumor microenvironment due to the feature of pH sensitive release. For Met, it was observed at pH 7.4 or pH 5.0 (Figure S2, B) that the nanoparticles showed a fast drug release rate. At pH 5.0, the released total amount of Met reached up to 82.86% at the first 24 h, and fast drug release rate was beneficial to reduce the tumoral O2 consumption, improve tumor oxygenation and subsequently enhance the efficiencies of hypoxia-caused cancer therapy.

First, the biocompatibility of BSA-HA-CDs and Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles was evaluated using MCF-7 and MCF-7/ADR cells by MTT test. As shown in Figure 4 , the results revealed that even after incubation with BSA-HA-CDs or Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles for 48 h, the survival rate of MCF-7 and MCF-7/ADR cells was still higher than 86% under hypoxia condition, demonstrating the excellent biocompatibility of these nanoparticles.

Next, in vitro antitumor efficiency of PDT, chemotherapy and synergistic therapy under hypoxia condition against MCF-7 and MCF-7/ADR cells was studied. In MCF-7 cells (Figure 4, A), the toxicity of BSA-HA-CDs + laser, Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser showed a time and CDs concentration-dependent manner, and the antitumor efficiency of Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser was higher than BSA-HA-CDs + laser. It might be attributed to Met improve relatively limited O2 in cancer cells, and increase the therapeutic efficiency of oxygen-consumed PDT. For chemotherapy, the toxicity of above three DOX formulations all showed concentration-dependent manner. Among these nanoparticles, the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs showed the strongest antitumor efficacy; the reason is that Met improved hypoxia-caused resistance and enhanced the therapeutic efficiency. For synergistic therapy, the toxicity of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser showed the most effective therapeutic efficacy when compared with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs. The results of MTT test toward MCF-7/ADR cells for 48 h under hypoxia condition indicated the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser exhibited the best outcome than PDT or chemotherapy (Figure 4, B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and evaluation of hypoxia
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the intracellular accumulation of drug, the red fluorescence of DOX with MCF-7 and MCF-7/ADR cells was observed by using the fluorescent microscope. The results were illustrated in Figure 5 . As shown in Figure 5, A, the higher red fluorescence signals were seen in DOX/Met/BSA-HA group, showing that the cellular uptake capacity of DOX was increased after load and delivery by nanoparticles. And the fluorescence of DOX/Met/BSA-HA was mainly distributed in the cell nucleus, which was explained by the fact that MCF-7 cells could quickly endocytose DOX/Met/BSA-HA nanoparticles by CD44 receptor. In MCF-7/ADR cells (Figure 5, B), due to the drug resistance, the fluorescent signal of free DOX was too weak to be detected after 12 h of incubation. This is significantly different with the centralized location of DOX in MCF-7 cell nucleus after incubation for 4 h (Figure 5, A). However, the fluorescent signal of DOX/Met/BSA-HA in MCF-7/ADR cells was significantly increased.

To further investigate the effect of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs toward hypoxia-induced drug resistance in MCF-7/ADR cells, western blotting was applied to detect the expression of HIF-1α and P-gp under normal and hypoxic conditions. As elucidated in Figure 5, C and D, the expression of HIF-1α and P-gp in hypoxia condition was higher than in normoxia condition, which indicated tumor hypoxia simulation was achieved. However, the expression of HIF-1α and P-gp was significantly decreased when treated with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs in hypoxia condition compared with control group, which resulted in the inhibited drug efflux and increased cellular DOX accumulation.

The red fluorescence of CDs for deeper tissue penetration is advantageous. Thus, in vivo real-time bio-imaging after i.v. injections of CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs was carried out to evaluate the whole-body distribution. Figure 6 , A showed the fluorescence images of CDs and DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs. For the CDs group, the fluorescence signals were widely distributed in the whole-body, and no specific localization was observed. By contrast, DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs group exhibited stronger fluorescence intensity in tumor regions, which peaked at 4 h and the fluorescence signals remained detectable at 24 h after the injection. These results confirmed CDs have great promise in fluorescence bio-imaging. To further verify the tumor-targeting effect of various formulations more accurately, ex vivo fluorescence images of tumor tissues and major organs were taken after 4 h post-injection (Figure 6, B). The fluorescence intensities of the organs were measured by Bruker data analysis software. As presented in Figure 6, C, compared with CDs group, the strongest fluorescence signal was detected from the excised tumor of the mice administered with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence of the tumor in the group of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs was remarkably higher than in other organs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs alleviate tumor hypoxia in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figure 7 , A, the tumor slices showed green fluorescence in the saline group, indicating that the tumor models were indeed hypoxic. Compared with the control group, the tumor slices treated with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs showed greatly reduced green fluorescence. Next, the semi-quantitative statistical analysis of the hypoxia areas (Figure 7, B) showed that the hypoxic tumor areas reduced from 40% in the saline group to 6% after 24 h injection with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs.

In vivo antitumor efficacy of the formulations was evaluated using S180 tumor model; the tumor sizes of mice during the treatment were monitored using a digital caliper every other day (Figure 8 , A). The tumor growth treated with BSA-HA-CDs and Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles showed no significantly difference with the saline group at either time point, which further indicated the good biocompatibility of this delivery platform and the low toxicity of Met. For PDT alone, the treatment with BSA-HA-CDs + laser group showed relatively small inhibition effect toward tumor, which might be attributed to hypoxic tumor microenvironment. However, in the group of treated by Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser, tumors grew slower than the BSA-HA-CDs + laser treated ones, indicating that Met could remit the hypoxia and improve the effect of PDT. For chemotherapy, the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs group showed preferable antitumor efficiency compared with the DOX and DOX/BSA-HA-CDs groups owing to their better accumulation and remitted the hypoxia-caused resistance. For synergistic therapy, the treatment group with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser displayed the best effective tumor growth inhibition efficacy of all the groups. The results were also confirmed by representative images of tumors after the treatments (Figure 8, D). The body weights of mice were recorded during the treatment (Figure 8, B); the mice had stable growth in body weights except for the DOX group, which demonstrated our nanoplatform can improve tumor inhibitory effect with insignificant side effects on the mice.

Furthermore, as shown in Figure 8, C, H&E staining analysis was also employed to assess the therapeutic efficacy and the systemic toxicity of these formulations. The saline group showed vigorous growth of tumor cells, while the mice treated with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser group revealed the superior treatment effect with widespread and serious apoptosis of the cancer cells. For DOX group, it showed obviously myocardial inflammation in the heart tissues, which might be attributed to the side effect of free DOX. By contrast, mice treated with saline, and various treatment groups showed no obvious signal of toxicity or damage in these major organs. These results demonstrated the safety of DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs and the potential clinical promise for cancer therapy.

In this paper, DOX and Met loaded BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles were produced for tumor diagnosis and therapy. Firstly, HA-CDs conjugates were synthesized by amide bond formation between carboxyl groups of HA and amine groups of CDs in water using EDC and NHS chemistry. Secondly, HA-CDs conjugates were attached to BSA performed by employing EDC and NHS, in which the reducing-end carbonyl group of HA was conjugated to the amino group of BSA. DOX and Met loaded BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles were produced via a desolvation crosslinking method. Subsequently, fluorescence properties of the CDs were further investigated. When the excitation wavelength was greater than 600 nm, CDs displayed strong emissions mainly at red-light region. In previous studies, 34 we demonstrated that the red emissive CDs can act as fluorescent imaging for cancer diagnosis in living mice. Therefore, the produced CDs can be applied for traceable imaging in vivo. Another important property of our obtained CDs is their photodynamic effects. In vitro ROS determination indicated that DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs could generate more ROS under the irradiation of 532 nm laser and have great potential for application in PDT. Next, the release manner of DOX and Met from DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles was monitored at different pH values (pH = 7.4 and 5.0) in PBS. Met showed a burst release, which was beneficial to reduce the tumoral O2 consumption, improve tumor oxygenation and subsequently enhance the efficiencies of hypoxia-caused cancer therapy. DOX was released in a sustained manner, thus the dual-drug system is advantageous for improving synergistic therapeutic efficacy.

The results of in vitro cytotoxicity assay toward MCF-7 and MCF-7/ADR cells showed the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser exhibited the best outcome than PDT or chemotherapy. The significantly therapeutic efficacy is attributed to the fact that Met could reduce O2 consumption which relieves hypoxic tumor microenvironment and subsequently improve the efficiency of synergistic PDT and chemotherapy. The results of cellular uptake indicated DOX/Met/BSA-HA nanoparticles could remarkably facilitate the cellular uptake of DOX, and finally DOX entered MCF-7/ADR cell nucleus to damage and kill tumor cells. The results of western blotting confirmed that DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs can effectively reduce O2 consumption and improve tumor cells hypoxia.

The results of in vivo real-time bio-imaging showed that the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanopaticles can effectively accumulate in the tumor region due to the specific affinity of HA with its receptor CD44. The results of ex vivo immunofluorescence staining indicated that the tumor hypoxia was successfully alleviated. The blood vessel density was not greatly different, which further indicated that the modulation of hypoxia by DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs is due to reduction in the tumor O2 consumption instead of affecting intratumor blood supply. Such improved tumor oxygenation would be in favor of enhancing hypoxia-associated PDT and decreasing chemotherapy resistance during in vivo. In vivo synergistic therapy indicated that the treatment group with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs + laser displayed the best effective tumor growth inhibition efficacy of all the groups; the outstanding therapeutic effect was attributed to the reduction of tumor O2 consumption which relieved tumor hypoxia.

In summary, we have fabricated a traceable nanocarrier for efficient chemo-photodynamic therapy of hypoxic cancer. The nanoparticles were composed of Met for reducing O2 consumption, CDs for PDT therapy and imaging-tracking, DOX for localized chemotherapy and BSA-HA-CDs for drug encapsulating and pH-responsive release. Utilizing the red fluorescence of CDs, it can be seen that the DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles showed efficient tumor accumulation. Furthermore, ex vivo immunofluorescence staining revealed that the oxygenation of tumors treated with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles was substantially greater than that in control mice. In vivo experiments demonstrated that tumors from mice injected with DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs nanoparticles were significantly reduced by synergistic PDT and chemotherapy. The results showed that Met can enhance the efficiency of combinational PDT and chemotherapy by reducing O2 consumption which relieves hypoxic tumor microenvironment. Overall, our study employed a very simple strategy to increase tumor oxygenation and apply to more effective synergistic anticancer therapy of hypoxic cancer.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article.
Supplementary Material
Image 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nano.2019.03.001.
